title: Sliding window max
description: Find maximum in sliding window using deque

examples.explanation:
Window 1: indices 0-2 = [1,3,-1], max = 3
Window 2: indices 1-3 = [3,-1,-3], max = 3
Window 3: indices 2-4 = [-1,-3,5], max = 5
Window 4: indices 3-5 = [-3,5,3], max = 5
Window 5: indices 4-6 = [5,3,6], max = 6
Window 6: indices 5-7 = [3,6,7], max = 7

Window 1: indices 0-1 = [5,4], max = 5
Window 2: indices 1-2 = [4,3], max = 4
Window 3: indices 2-3 = [3,2], max = 3
Window 4: indices 3-4 = [2,1], max = 2

approaches.name: Brute Force O(nk)
approaches.summary: For each sliding window position, iterate through all k elements to find the maximum. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sliding Window Maximum with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding maximum in each sliding window by scanning all k elements for each window position.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to the sliding window maximum problem is conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The intuition is straightforward: for each possible starting position of the window, we examine all k elements within that window to find the maximum value. This works because each window is independent - the maximum in one window doesn't depend on the maximum in previous windows. The algorithm proceeds by iterating through all possible window starting positions from 0 to n-k, where n is the array length and k is the window size. For each starting position i, we initialize a variable to track the maximum, then iterate through indices i to i+k-1 to find the maximum element. This maximum is then added to the result list. The time complexity is O(nk) because we have n-k+1 windows, each requiring O(k) operations to find the maximum. Space complexity is O(1) for the algorithm itself (excluding the output array). This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in window boundaries, forgetting to handle edge cases like k=1 or k=n, and incorrectly calculating the number of windows. The real pattern involves nested loops where the outer loop moves the window and the inner loop scans the window contents.

examples.trace:
Window 1: indices 0-2 = [1,3,-1], max = 3
Window 2: indices 1-3 = [3,-1,-3], max = 3
Window 3: indices 2-4 = [-1,-3,5], max = 5
Window 4: indices 3-5 = [-3,5,3], max = 5
Window 5: indices 4-6 = [5,3,6], max = 6
Window 6: indices 5-7 = [3,6,7], max = 7

Window 1: indices 0-1 = [5,4], max = 5
Window 2: indices 1-2 = [4,3], max = 4
Window 3: indices 2-3 = [3,2], max = 3
Window 4: indices 3-4 = [2,1], max = 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Maximum in a Fixed Window
ladders.desc: Implement a function that finds the maximum value in a given subarray of fixed length k.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach - finding the maximum element within a specific window of the array. The intuition is simple: given a starting index and window size, we need to examine each element in that contiguous segment and keep track of the largest value encountered. This works because finding the maximum in a collection requires comparing each element against the current maximum. The algorithm takes an array, a starting index, and window size k as input. It initializes a variable to store the maximum, typically starting with the first element of the window. Then it iterates through the remaining k-1 elements, updating the maximum whenever a larger value is found. The time complexity is O(k) since we examine each of the k elements exactly once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This sub-routine connects to the full problem by being called for each window position. Common mistakes include not handling empty windows, incorrect initialization of the maximum value, and off-by-one errors in the loop bounds. The real pattern is a simple linear scan through a subarray while maintaining a running maximum.

examples.trace:
Window indices: 2,3,4 = [-1,-3,5]
Initialize max = nums[2] = -1
Compare with nums[3] = -3: -3 < -1, max remains -1
Compare with nums[4] = 5: 5 > -1, update max = 5
Final max = 5

Window indices: 1,2 = [4,3]
Initialize max = nums[1] = 4
Compare with nums[2] = 3: 3 < 4, max remains 4
Final max = 4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Through All Window Positions
ladders.desc: Generate all possible starting positions for a sliding window of size k in an array of length n.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the sliding window maximum problem involves systematically moving a fixed-size window across the array. The intuition is that for an array of length n and window size k, there are exactly n-k+1 possible windows, each starting at a different position. This works because the window must fit entirely within the array bounds. The algorithm calculates the number of windows as n-k+1, then iterates from 0 to n-k (inclusive) to generate each starting index. For each starting position i, the window covers indices i through i+k-1. This systematic progression ensures we examine every possible contiguous subarray of length k. The time complexity is O(n-k+1) for generating the positions, though this is typically dominated by the work done within each window. Space complexity is O(1). This logic connects to the key sub-routine by providing the starting positions for which we need to find maximums. Common mistakes include incorrect calculation of the number of windows (using n-k instead of n-k+1), off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, and not handling the case where k > n. The real pattern is a simple for loop with well-defined start and end conditions based on array length and window size.

examples.trace:
Number of windows = 8-3+1 = 6
Window 0: indices 0-2
Window 1: indices 1-3
Window 2: indices 2-4
Window 3: indices 3-5
Window 4: indices 4-6
Window 5: indices 5-7

Number of windows = 5-2+1 = 4
Window 0: indices 0-1
Window 1: indices 1-2
Window 2: indices 2-3
Window 3: indices 3-4

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Slicing and Indexing
ladders.desc: Access and manipulate contiguous subarrays using index ranges.
ladders.explanation: Array slicing and indexing form the fundamental building block for working with sliding windows. The intuition is that a sliding window corresponds to a contiguous segment of an array, which can be accessed using a range of indices. This works because arrays provide constant-time access to any element given its index. The algorithm involves understanding how to specify a subarray using a start index and length: elements from index 'start' to 'start+k-1' (inclusive) form the window. We need to ensure all indices are within the array bounds (0 to n-1). The key operations include accessing individual elements (array[i]), iterating through a range (for i in range(start, start+k)), and understanding inclusive/exclusive bounds. Time complexity for accessing a subarray is O(k) if we examine all elements. Space complexity is O(1) if we just access elements without copying. This connects to the core logic by providing the mechanism to examine window contents. Common mistakes include confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, incorrect calculation of the end index (using start+k instead of start+k-1), and accessing indices outside array bounds. The real pattern involves careful index arithmetic and boundary checking.

examples.trace:
Start index = 2, k = 3
End index = 2+3-1 = 4
Indices to access: 2,3,4
nums[2] = -1
nums[3] = -3
nums[4] = 5
Result: [-1,-3,5]

Start index = 1, k = 2
End index = 1+2-1 = 2
Indices to access: 1,2
nums[1] = 4
nums[2] = 3
Result: [4,3]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Maximum in a Collection
ladders.desc: Implement the fundamental operation of finding the largest element in a given set of numbers.
ladders.explanation: Finding the maximum value in a collection is a fundamental operation that underpins the sliding window maximum problem. The intuition is simple: we need to compare elements pairwise to determine which is largest. This works because the maximum is defined as the element that is greater than or equal to all other elements in the collection. The algorithm typically initializes a variable with the first element (or negative infinity if the collection might be empty), then iterates through the remaining elements, updating the maximum whenever a larger value is encountered. For an array of m elements, this requires m-1 comparisons. The time complexity is O(m) linear time, and space complexity is O(1) constant space. This basic operation connects to array slicing by being applied to the subarray representing each window. Common mistakes include not handling empty collections, incorrectly initializing the maximum (e.g., with 0 when negative numbers are possible), and using the wrong comparison operator. The real pattern is a simple loop with a comparison and update mechanism.

examples.trace:
Initialize max = first element = 3
Compare with 1: 1 < 3, max remains 3
Compare with 4: 4 > 3, update max = 4
Compare with 1: 1 < 4, max remains 4
Compare with 5: 5 > 4, update max = 5
Final max = 5

Initialize max = first element = -2
Compare with -5: -5 < -2, max remains -2
Compare with -1: -1 > -2, update max = -1
Compare with -3: -3 < -1, max remains -1
Final max = -1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal with Loops
ladders.desc: Understand how to iterate through array elements using index-based loops.
ladders.explanation: Array traversal using loops is the most basic prerequisite for the sliding window maximum problem. The intuition is that to process each element in an array, we need to systematically visit them one by one. This works because arrays are ordered collections with indices that allow sequential access. The algorithm involves using a loop variable (typically called i) that takes on each valid index value from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length. In each iteration, we can access the element at position i using array[i]. For an array of length n, this requires exactly n iterations. The time complexity is O(n) linear time, and space complexity is O(1) if we only access elements without creating new data structures. This foundation connects to finding the maximum by providing the mechanism to examine each element. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (going beyond array bounds), confusing array indices with element values, and not understanding 0-based indexing. The real pattern is a simple for or while loop that increments an index variable until it reaches the array length.

examples.trace:
Initialize i = 0
Iteration 1: i=0, nums[0]=1
Iteration 2: i=1, nums[1]=2
Iteration 3: i=2, nums[2]=3
Iteration 4: i=3, nums[3]=4
Iteration 5: i=4, nums[4]=5
Loop ends as i=5 >= array length 5

Initialize i = 0
Iteration 1: i=0, nums[0]=10
Iteration 2: i=1, nums[1]=20
Iteration 3: i=2, nums[2]=30
Loop ends as i=3 >= array length 3

approaches.name: Heap O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Use a max-heap to track elements in the current window, removing elements as they slide out. This leverages efficient maximum retrieval but requires lazy deletion for out-of-window elements.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sliding Window Maximum with Max-Heap
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a max-heap with lazy deletion to find maximums in all sliding windows of size k.
ladders.explanation: The heap-based approach solves the sliding window maximum problem by maintaining a max-heap of elements in the current window. The key insight is that we can't simply pop elements from the heap when they slide out of the window because they might not be at the root. Instead, we use lazy deletion: we mark elements as invalid when they leave the window and only remove them when they reach the heap's root. This ensures O(n log k) time complexity. The algorithm works by: 1) Adding each new element to the heap with its index, 2) Checking if the root element is outside the current window, 3) If so, repeatedly popping until we find a valid root, 4) Recording the root's value as the current window's maximum. The intuition is that a max-heap gives us O(1) access to the maximum, but we need O(log k) to maintain it. Lazy deletion avoids the O(k) cost of removing arbitrary elements. The space is O(k) for the heap. Connection to L1: This builds on the lazy deletion mechanism to handle the full sliding window traversal. Common mistakes include: not storing indices with values (making lazy deletion impossible), forgetting to check window boundaries at each step, and not handling duplicate values correctly (indices distinguish them). Real code pattern: Use heapq with negative values for max-heap in Python, store (negative_value, index) tuples.

examples.trace:
Initialize heap = [], result = [].
Step 1-3: Add elements for i=0,1,2. Heap after i=2: [(-7,2), (-8,1), (-9,0)]. Window size < k.
Step 4 (i=3): Add (-6,3). Heap = [(-7,2), (-8,1), (-9,0), (-6,3)]. Window [0,3] complete. Root (-7,2) valid, max = 7? Wait, max-heap with negatives: root is smallest negative = largest value. Actually heap stores (-value, index). So root (-9,0) gives max 9. Let's trace correctly: After i=0: heap = [(-9,0)]; i=1: push (-8,1), heap = [(-9,0), (-8,1)]; i=2: push (-7,2), heap = [(-9,0), (-8,1), (-7,2)]; i=3: push (-6,3), heap = [(-9,0), (-8,1), (-7,2), (-6,3)]. Window [0,3]. Root (-9,0) valid, max = 9. Result = [9].
Step 5 (i=4): Add (-5,4). Heap = [(-9,0), (-8,1), (-7,2), (-6,3), (-5,4)]. Window [1,4]. Root (-9,0) invalid (index 0 < left=1), pop. New heap after pop: [(-8,1), (-6,3), (-7,2), (-5,4)]. Root (-8,1) valid, max = 8. Result = [9,8].
Step 6 (i=5): Add (-4,5). Heap = [(-8,1), (-6,3), (-7,2), (-5,4), (-4,5)]. Window [2,5]. Root (-8,1) invalid (index 1 < left=2), pop. New heap: [(-7,2), (-6,3), (-4,5), (-5,4)]. Root (-7,2) valid, max = 7. Result = [9,8,7].
Step 7 (i=6): Add (-3,6). Heap = [(-7,2), (-6,3), (-4,5), (-5,4), (-3,6)]. Window [3,6]. Root (-7,2) invalid (index 2 < left=3), pop. New heap: [(-6,3), (-5,4), (-4,5), (-3,6)]. Root (-6,3) valid, max = 6. Result = [9,8,7,6].
Step 8 (i=7): Add (-2,7). Heap = [(-6,3), (-5,4), (-4,5), (-3,6), (-2,7)]. Window [4,7]. Root (-6,3) invalid (index 3 < left=4), pop. New heap: [(-5,4), (-3,6), (-4,5), (-2,7)]. Root (-5,4) valid, max = 5. Result = [9,8,7,6,5].
Step 9 (i=8): Add (-1,8). Window [5,8] gives max 4, but output stops at 5 elements since n=9, k=4 gives n-k+1=6 windows? Actually n=9, k=4: windows 0-3,1-4,2-5,3-6,4-7,5-8 = 6 windows. Continue: Add (-1,8), heap = [(-5,4), (-3,6), (-4,5), (-2,7), (-1,8)]. Window [5,8]. Root (-5,4) invalid (index 4 < left=5), pop. New heap: [(-4,5), (-3,6), (-1,8), (-2,7)]. Root (-4,5) valid, max = 4. Result = [9,8,7,6,5,4]. But expected output is [9,8,7,6,5]? Let's verify: Input nums length 9, k=4, output should have 6 elements. My error in example statement. Correct output should be [9,8,7,6,5,4]. I'll correct in test cases.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Lazy Deletion in Max-Heap
ladders.desc: Implement lazy deletion to remove out-of-window elements from a max-heap efficiently.
ladders.explanation: Lazy deletion is the critical sub-routine that makes the heap approach work for sliding windows. Instead of immediately removing elements when they exit the window (which would require O(k) time to find and remove arbitrary elements from a heap), we mark them as invalid and only remove them when they naturally rise to the root of the heap. This works because: 1) We store each element as a tuple (value, index) in the heap, 2) We track the current window's left boundary, 3) When we need the current maximum, we check if the root's index is within the window, 4) If not, we pop it (O(log k)) and repeat until the root is valid. The intuition is that invalid elements don't interfere with finding the maximum as long as they're not at the root. This reduces removal cost from O(k) to amortized O(log k). The algorithm: maintain a max-heap (using negative values in min-heap), and at each step: while heap is not empty and heap[0][1] < left_index: heapq.heappop(heap). Time: Each element is pushed once and popped at most once, so O(n log k) amortized. Space: O(k). Connection to L0: This mechanism enables the full sliding window solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to store indices, not using a while loop (multiple invalid roots possible), and incorrect comparison (checking index <= left instead of < left). Real code pattern: Use heapq module with (negative_value, index).

examples.trace: Initial heap (min-heap with negatives): [(-5,0), (-3,1), (-4,2)]. Root is (-5,0) (smallest negative = largest value 5). Check root index 0 < left=2? Yes, invalid. Pop root. New heap after pop: [(-4,2), (-3,1)]. Root (-4,2). Check index 2 < left=2? No (equal), valid. Root value = 4 (since -4 gives 4).
Initial heap: [(-9,0), (-8,1), (-7,2), (-6,3)]. Root (-9,0). Index 0 < left=1? Yes, invalid. Pop. New heap: [(-8,1), (-6,3), (-7,2)]. Root (-8,1). Index 1 < left=1? No, valid. Root value = 8.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Maximum in a Sliding Window with Heap
ladders.desc: Use a max-heap to track the maximum element as the window slides, handling window boundaries.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves using a max-heap to efficiently retrieve the maximum element in the current window. As the window slides one position to the right, we add the new element to the heap and need to ensure the heap's root represents a valid maximum. The challenge is that elements leave the window from the left, but they might not be at the heap root. The solution is to couple each heap element with its index and use lazy deletion. At each step: 1) Push the new element (with its index) onto the heap, 2) Remove invalid elements from the root using lazy deletion, 3) The root now gives the current maximum. The intuition is that the heap maintains all window elements sorted by value, and lazy deletion cleans up outdated ones without expensive searches. Time: O(log k) per insertion and amortized O(log k) per deletion. Space: O(k). Connection to L1: This uses lazy deletion as a tool. Common mistakes: Not updating the window left boundary correctly, assuming heap root is always valid without checking, and mishandling when the window first fills up. Real code pattern: For i in range(n): push; if i >= k-1: lazy delete; append root value.

examples.input: Window elements: [3,1,2] added in order, then slide to [1,2,4]
examples.trace:
Start with empty heap. Add 3: heap = [(-3,0)]. Add 1: heap = [(-3,0), (-1,1)]. Add 2: heap = [(-3,0), (-1,1), (-2,2)]. Window full [0,2]. Lazy delete with left=0 (no invalid). Root (-3,0) gives max 3.
Slide: New element 4 at index 3. Add (-4,3): heap = [(-4,3), (-3,0), (-2,2), (-1,1)]. New window [1,3]. Lazy delete: left=1. Root (-4,3) index 3 >=1, valid. Max = 4.

Add 5: heap = [(-5,0)]. Add 3: heap = [(-5,0), (-3,1)]. Add 4: heap = [(-5,0), (-3,1), (-4,2)]. Window [0,2]. Root (-5,0) valid, max 5.
Slide: Add 1 at index 3: heap = [(-5,0), (-3,1), (-4,2), (-1,3)]. New window [1,3]. Lazy delete: left=1. Root (-5,0) index 0 <1, invalid. Pop. New heap: [(-4,2), (-3,1), (-1,3)]. Root (-4,2) index 2 >=1, valid. Max = 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Max-Heap Operations with Indices
ladders.desc: Implement a max-heap that stores (value, index) pairs to support indexed access.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a max-heap where each element is a tuple containing a value and its index. In Python, heaps are min-heaps by default, so we store negative values to simulate a max-heap. The index is crucial for lazy deletion because it lets us identify when an element is out of the window. Operations needed: push (value, index) onto heap, peek at root, pop root, and check root's index. The intuition is that the heap orders elements by value (largest first due to negation), and the index is metadata that doesn't affect ordering but is stored alongside. Time: O(log n) for push/pop, O(1) for peek. Space: O(n). Connection to L2: This data structure enables the core logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to negate values for max-heap, storing index first (which would order by index), and not handling duplicate values correctly (indices distinguish them). Real code pattern: Use heapq.heappush(heap, (-value, index)) and heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace:
Start empty heap.
Push 5: heapq.heappush(heap, (-5,0)). Heap = [(-5,0)].
Push 3: heapq.heappush(heap, (-3,1)). Heap = [(-5,0), (-3,1)].
Push 8: heapq.heappush(heap, (-8,2)). Heap adjusts: compare (-5,0) and (-8,2), -8 < -5, so swap. Heap = [(-8,2), (-5,0), (-3,1)].
Initial heap: [(-9,0), (-7,1), (-8,2)]. Root is (-9,0). Pop: remove (-9,0), heap becomes [(-7,1), (-8,2)]. Reheapify: compare children, -8 < -7, so swap. Final heap: [(-8,2), (-7,1)].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Heap Push and Pop with Tuples
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental heap operations (push and pop) on tuples in a min-heap.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic heap operations using Python's heapq module, which implements a min-heap. Pushing and popping tuples works by comparing the first element of the tuple, then the second if tied, etc. For example, pushing (3,0) and (2,1) results in heap [(2,1), (3,0)] because 2 < 3. Popping removes the smallest tuple. The intuition is that heaps maintain partial order for efficient minimum retrieval. Time: O(log n) for push/pop. Space: O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This is how we implement the max-heap with indices. Common mistakes: Not using heapq functions directly (e.g., trying to sort manually), misunderstanding tuple ordering, and not handling empty heap pops. Real code pattern: heapq.heappush(heap, item); heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace:
Start empty heap.
Push (5,0): heap = [(5,0)].
Push (3,1): compare 3 < 5, so swap. Heap = [(3,1), (5,0)].
Push (7,2): compare 7 > 3 and 7 > 5, no swap. Heap = [(3,1), (5,0), (7,2)].

Initial heap: [(1,0), (4,1), (2,2)].
Pop: remove (1,0), heap becomes [(2,2), (4,1)].
Pop: remove (2,2), heap becomes [(4,1)].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Heaps and Priority Queues
ladders.desc: Learn what a heap is and how it provides efficient access to maximum or minimum elements.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree data structure that satisfies the heap property: in a min-heap, each node's value is less than or equal to its children's values, giving O(1) access to the minimum element. In a max-heap, each node's value is greater than or equal to its children's, giving O(1) access to the maximum. Heaps are often used to implement priority queues, where elements are processed based on priority (value). Key operations: insert (push) takes O(log n) by adding at the end and bubbling up; remove root (pop) takes O(log n) by replacing root with last element and bubbling down. The intuition is that heaps trade exact sorting for efficient priority access. Time: O(log n) for insert/delete, O(1) for peek. Space: O(n). Connection to L4: This foundational knowledge enables heap operations. Common mistakes: Confusing heap with sorted array (heap is not fully sorted), forgetting it's a tree structure, and not understanding the log n complexity. Real code pattern: In Python, use list with heapq module for min-heap.

examples.output: Heap structure: 3 at root, children 5 and 8
examples.trace:
Start empty.
Insert 5: heap = [5].
Insert 3: add 3 at end, compare with parent 5, 3 < 5, swap. Heap = [3,5].
Insert 8: add 8 at end, compare with parent 3, 8 > 3, no swap. Heap = [3,5,8]. Tree: root 3, left child 5, right child 8.

Initial heap: [2,4,3].
Pop root 2: replace with last element 3, heap becomes [3,4].
Bubble down: compare 3 with children (only 4), 3 < 4, no swap. Final heap: [3,4].

approaches.name: Deque O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a deque that stores indices in decreasing order of values, ensuring the front always has the maximum for the current window. This efficiently handles sliding by removing outdated indices.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sliding Window Maximum with Deque
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find maximum values in all sliding windows of size k using a deque.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum value in every contiguous subarray (window) of size k as it slides through an array of length n. The brute force approach would be O(n*k) by checking each window separately. The deque approach achieves O(n) by maintaining a data structure that efficiently tracks potential maximum candidates. The key insight is that we only need to keep elements in decreasing order within the deque, since once a larger element appears, smaller elements to its left can never be the maximum for future windows. We store indices in the deque rather than values to easily check if elements are outside the current window. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty deque and result list. 2) For each index i from 0 to n-1: a) Remove from front if index is outside window (i-k). b) Remove from back while current element is greater than deque's back element. c) Add current index to back. d) If window is complete (i ≥ k-1), add front element's value to result. Time: O(n) - each element added/removed at most once. Space: O(k) for deque storage. Connection: This integrates all sub-components into complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to remove outdated indices, incorrect window boundary checks, storing values instead of indices. Real pattern: deque = collections.deque(); result = []; for i, num in enumerate(nums): if deque and deque[0] <= i-k: deque.popleft(); while deque and nums[deque[-1]] <= num: deque.pop(); deque.append(i); if i >= k-1: result.append(nums[deque[0]]).

examples.trace:
i=0, num=1: deque=[], window incomplete
i=1, num=3: remove nothing, deque=[0], remove 0 since 3>1, deque=[], add 1, deque=[1], window incomplete
i=2, num=-1: check front 1 valid, remove from back? -1<3 so keep, add 2, deque=[1,2], window complete, result=[3]
i=3, num=-3: front 1 valid, remove back? -3<-1 so keep, add 3, deque=[1,2,3], result=[3,3]
i=4, num=5: front 1 invalid (4-3=1), remove 1, deque=[2,3], remove 2,3 since 5>-1,-3, deque=[], add 4, deque=[4], result=[3,3,5]
i=5, num=3: front 4 valid, remove back? 3<5 so keep, add 5, deque=[4,5], result=[3,3,5,5]
i=6, num=6: front 4 invalid (6-3=3), remove 4, deque=[5], remove 5 since 6>3, deque=[], add 6, deque=[6], result=[3,3,5,5,6]
i=7, num=7: front 6 valid, remove back? 7>6 so remove 6, deque=[], add 7, deque=[7], result=[3,3,5,5,6,7]

i=0, num=9: deque=[], add 0, window incomplete
i=1, num=8: front valid, remove back? 8<9 so keep, add 1, deque=[0,1], incomplete
i=2, num=7: front valid, remove back? 7<8 so keep, add 2, deque=[0,1,2], incomplete
i=3, num=6: front valid, remove back? 6<7 so keep, add 3, deque=[0,1,2,3], window complete, result=[9]
i=4, num=5: front 0 invalid (4-4=0), remove 0, deque=[1,2,3], remove back? 5<6 so keep, add 4, deque=[1,2,3,4], result=[9,8]
i=5, num=4: front 1 invalid (5-4=1), remove 1, deque=[2,3,4], remove back? 4<5 so keep, add 5, deque=[2,3,4,5], result=[9,8,7]
i=6, num=3: front 2 invalid (6-4=2), remove 2, deque=[3,4,5], remove back? 3<4 so keep, add 6, deque=[3,4,5,6], result=[9,8,7,6]
i=7, num=2: front 3 invalid (7-4=3), remove 3, deque=[4,5,6], remove back? 2<3 so keep, add 7, deque=[4,5,6,7], result=[9,8,7,6,5]
i=8, num=1: front 4 invalid (8-4=4), remove 4, deque=[5,6,7], remove back? 1<2 so keep, add 8, deque=[5,6,7,8], result=[9,8,7,6,5,4]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Decreasing Order in Deque
ladders.desc: Implement the core deque maintenance logic that keeps indices in decreasing value order.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the sliding window maximum algorithm. Given a deque storing indices (initially empty) and a new element with index i and value nums[i], we need to update the deque to maintain the property that indices are stored in decreasing order of their corresponding values. The intuition: When we encounter a new element, any existing elements in the deque with smaller values than the new element become irrelevant for future maximum calculations, since the new larger element will dominate them in any window containing both. We process from the back of the deque because we want to preserve the decreasing order property. Algorithm: 1) While deque is not empty AND nums[deque[-1]] <= nums[i]: remove from back. 2) Append i to deque. This ensures the front always has the largest value's index among stored indices. Time: O(1) amortized per operation (each index added/removed once). Space: O(k). Connection: This sub-routine is called for each element in L0. Common mistakes: Using < instead of <= (important for equal values), comparing values incorrectly, not handling empty deque. Real pattern: while deque and nums[deque[-1]] <= nums[i]: deque.pop(); deque.append(i).

examples.input: Current deque indices: [0,2] with values [5,3], new element index 3 value 4
examples.trace:
Start: deque=[0,2], values=[5,3], new i=3, val=4
Check back: deque[-1]=2, nums[2]=3 ≤ 4? Yes, remove 2, deque=[0]
Check back: deque[-1]=0, nums[0]=5 ≤ 4? No, stop
Append 3: deque=[0,3], values now [5,4] (decreasing)

examples.input: Current deque indices: [1,3,4] with values [8,6,5], new element index 5 value 9
examples.trace:
Start: deque=[1,3,4], values=[8,6,5], new i=5, val=9
Check back: deque[-1]=4, nums[4]=5 ≤ 9? Yes, remove 4, deque=[1,3]
Check back: deque[-1]=3, nums[3]=6 ≤ 9? Yes, remove 3, deque=[1]
Check back: deque[-1]=1, nums[1]=8 ≤ 9? Yes, remove 1, deque=[]
Append 5: deque=[5], values [9]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Why Decreasing Order Ensures Maximum Tracking
ladders.desc: Understand the mathematical insight that maintaining decreasing order in deque guarantees front has maximum.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is maintaining a deque where indices are stored in strictly decreasing order of their corresponding values. Why does this work? Consider any window position: the maximum element must be the first element in the deque because: 1) By construction, we remove smaller elements when a larger one arrives, so the front has the largest value among all stored indices. 2) We only remove elements from the front when they slide out of the window, ensuring the front is always within the current window. 3) The decreasing order property means that if the front element leaves the window, the next element is the next largest, ready to become the new maximum. This is efficient because we avoid storing unnecessary elements—once a larger element appears, smaller ones to its left can never be the maximum for future windows containing the larger element. The deque thus stores only 'candidate' maximums. Step-by-step: For each new element, remove from back all indices with values ≤ current value (these become obsolete), then add current index. This preserves decreasing order. Time: O(1) amortized per element. Space: O(k). Connection: This logic is implemented in L1's sub-routine. Common mistakes: Not understanding why we remove from back, confusing with increasing order. Real pattern: The deque represents a sequence where each new element 'dominates' previous smaller ones.

examples.input: Window positions with values [5,3,4,2], show deque evolution
examples.trace:
i=0 val=5: deque=[0] (5)
i=1 val=3: 3≤5? No, keep, deque=[0,1] (5,3 decreasing)
i=2 val=4: Check back: 3≤4? Yes, remove 1, deque=[0], check: 5≤4? No, add 2, deque=[0,2] (5,4 decreasing)
i=3 val=2: Check back: 4≤2? No, add 3, deque=[0,2,3] (5,4,2 decreasing). Front always max.

examples.input: Values [2,1,3,4], show how 4 removes previous candidates
examples.trace:
i=0 val=2: deque=[0]
i=1 val=1: 1≤2? No, deque=[0,1] (2,1)
i=2 val=3: Check back: 1≤3? Yes, remove 1, deque=[0], check: 2≤3? Yes, remove 0, deque=[], add 2, deque=[2] (3)
i=3 val=4: Check back: 3≤4? Yes, remove 2, deque=[], add 3, deque=[3] (4). Larger values clear smaller ones.

ladders.title: Building Block — Efficient Deque Operations for Window Management
ladders.desc: Learn to use deque for adding/removing elements from both ends to maintain window constraints.
ladders.explanation: A deque (double-ended queue) allows O(1) insertion and deletion at both front and back, making it ideal for sliding window problems. Here, we use it to: 1) Remove elements from the front when their indices fall outside the current window (i.e., index ≤ i-k). This ensures only relevant indices remain. 2) Remove elements from the back when new larger elements make them obsolete (as in L2). 3) Add new indices to the back. The intuition: The front represents the current maximum candidate, while the back is where we add new elements and remove smaller ones. By checking the front index against i-k, we efficiently slide the window without scanning all elements. Step-by-step for each index i: Check if deque[0] ≤ i-k, if yes, popleft(). Then process back operations. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(k). Connection: This window management combined with L2's order logic gives L1. Common mistakes: Incorrect inequality (using < instead of ≤), not checking if deque is empty before accessing. Real pattern: if deque and deque[0] <= i-k: deque.popleft().

examples.output: Remove front if ≤ 1, deque becomes [2,3] if 1 removed
examples.trace: Current i=4, k=3, so i-k=1. Check front: deque[0]=1. Is 1 ≤ 1? Yes, so remove front (popleft). Deque becomes [2,3]. This removes index 1 because window now covers indices 2,3,4.
i=5, k=2, i-k=3. Check front: deque[0]=4. Is 4 ≤ 3? No, so keep. Deque remains [4]. Index 4 is still in window [4,5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Adding and Removing from Deque Ends
ladders.desc: Practice fundamental deque operations: append to back, pop from back, popleft from front.
ladders.explanation: A deque supports constant-time operations at both ends, which is crucial for our algorithm. Basic operations: 1) append(x): Add element x to the right end (back). 2) pop(): Remove and return element from the right end (back). 3) popleft(): Remove and return element from the left end (front). In Python, collections.deque provides these. The intuition: We use append to add new indices, pop to remove smaller values from the back when a larger element arrives, and popleft to remove outdated indices from the front as the window slides. This allows efficient maintenance without shifting elements. Step-by-step: Initialize empty deque. For adding: deque.append(i). For removing from back: while condition: deque.pop(). For removing from front: if condition: deque.popleft(). Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(n) for storage. Connection: These operations are used in L3 for window management. Common mistakes: Using list operations (O(n) for front removal), confusing pop() with popleft(). Real pattern: from collections import deque; d = deque(); d.append(1); d.pop(); d.popleft().

examples.input: Start with deque [2,4,6], perform: append(8), pop(), popleft()
examples.trace:
Initial: deque=[2,4,6]
append(8): add to back, deque=[2,4,6,8]
pop(): remove from back (8), deque=[2,4,6]
popleft(): remove from front (2), deque=[4,6]

examples.input: Empty deque, perform: append(1), append(3), popleft(), append(5)
examples.trace:
Start: deque=[]
append(1): deque=[1]
append(3): deque=[1,3]
popleft(): remove front (1), deque=[3]
append(5): deque=[3,5]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sliding Windows
ladders.desc: Learn the basic idea of a fixed-size window moving through an array.
ladders.explanation: A sliding window is a contiguous subarray of fixed size k that moves one step at a time through a larger array. For an array of length n, there are n-k+1 such windows. Example: array [1,2,3,4,5] with k=3 has windows [1,2,3], [2,3,4], [3,4,5]. The concept is used in many problems to process subsets efficiently without recomputing from scratch. Intuition: As the window slides, one element leaves (leftmost) and one enters (rightmost), allowing incremental updates. Step-by-step: Start with first k elements as window 0. For each step i from k to n-1, window becomes elements from i-k+1 to i. This creates a sequence of overlapping subarrays. Time to list all windows: O(n) if done incrementally. Space: O(k) for current window. Connection: This foundational concept is why we need to track maximums as windows slide in L4+. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, misunderstanding window count. Real pattern: for i in range(k, n): window = arr[i-k+1 : i+1].

examples.trace:
n=4, k=2, number of windows = 4-2+1=3
Window 0: indices 0-1 → [10,20]
Window 1: indices 1-2 → [20,30]
Window 2: indices 2-3 → [30,40]

n=4, k=3, windows = 2
Window 0: indices 0-2 → [5,1,7]
Window 1: indices 1-3 → [1,7,3]
